C-41795-Ba polypeptide showed no significant difference in its activity in producing 11-OHCS as compared with tetracosactrin when given in doses of 100 and 200 micrograms per kilogram body weight. To evaluate the efficacy and safety of C-41795-Ba, a total of 17 normal young males were enrolled in this study. All participants were given either 100 or 200 micrograms of C-41795-Ba per kilogram of body weight, depending on the dosage group they were assigned to. The commercially available tetracosactrin was used as a control substance. texts.full_text: ago I used sudo update-alternatives --install /usr/bin/python python /usr/bin/python3 10 to simply write python for having python3. I noticed this alias breaks the package manager in some cases, so Im trying to revert this. I used sudo update-alternatives --remove-all python to remove this. But now I want to go back to default, meaning writing python calls python2 and only python3 calls python3. Any idea how we can do this? Cheers EDIT: By running sudo update-alternatives --install /usr/bin/python python /usr/bin/python2 10 I managed to link python2 to python and leaving python3 as it. is. Is this the proper way of fixing it? It doesn't seem like it... EDIT2: I just upgraded my system from 19.1To set Python 2 as the default when you run `python` and keep Python 3 accessible via `python3`, you can use the `update-alternatives` command as follows: ```bash sudo update-alternatives --install /usr/bin/python python /usr/bin/python2 10 ``` Then, to set Python 2 as the default: ```bash sudo update-alternatives --config python ``` When prompted, select the option corresponding to Python 2. This will: 1. Install Python 2 as an alternative for the `python` command. 2. Configure the system to use Python 2 by default when you run `python`. After doing this, you can still access Python 3 by explicitly using `python3`. Note: Ensure that both Python 2 and Python 3 are installed on your system. texts.full_text: with the one represented in Diego Riveivera' fresco. This remarkable intersection between historical science and artistic expression offers us unique insight into Morgagni's innovative approach to medical diagnosis through the symptoms presented by patients rather than relying solely on anatomical dissection. Morgani, as he is commonly referred after his Italian name Giovanni Battista (1708 - 1771), was a pioneer in clinical medicine during what we now call "the age of clinical investigation". His work marked one of the most significant shifts towards understanding disease mechanisms. Unlike the preceding anatomists who investigated dissection specimens, Morgagni focused on symptoms and their relation to specific diseases through examination. Diego Rivera's depiction within his fresco honors this transformation in medical thought by capturing important moments from Morgagni’s professional career at Padua Medical School where these principles were applied. His clinical method is now referred as “auscultatory diagnosis”, a technique based on the concept that understanding diseases required listening to what patients told us. It's worth noting how Vesalius laid down foundational anatomical truths for generations of physicians while Harvey’s circulation experiments fundamentally changed our view of physiology. But Morgagni transcended these foundations by connecting clinical observations with symptomatology, a critical step in evolving modern diagnostic medicine – one vividly represented through Diego Rivera's artistry. Reading De sedibus from the Paduan master alongside the fresco allows us to meditate on how Rivera’s vision of Morgagni was not just visually engaging but also intellectually enlightening—synthesizing historical medical science, artistic interpretation and didactic education in a powerful visual narrative. The case described pertains specifically to aneurysm cases; however, the fresco encompasses much more – capturing significant milestones from the history of medicine while recognizing Morgagni's monumental contribution through Rivera’s masterful work. But, when it called twice I see no content! After some investigation, I found that problem's caused by this: setContentView(R.layout.fragment_layout); E.g., when it is called once, fragment content is perfectly shown! But I need to call setContentView a lot of times to show another fragments. Where's the mistake? P.S. It's possible to make MyListFragment hardcoded into XML, but this does not fit to me, because I need to replace layout contents witThe issue you are encountering is related to how Android manages Fragments and their lifecycle within an Activity or FragmentActivity when using a FrameLayout as the container for your fragments. Here's what might be happening: 1.First Call: When `setContentView` method sets fragment_layout, which contains only one `<Frame Layout>` element with id `@+id/items`, it creates this layout and associates any future Fragments to that specific view using its ID as a reference point for replacement. The code then checks if the 'MyListFragment' exists in your FragmentActivity's `getSupportManager().findFragemntByTag` method, which initially returns null because you are creating an instance of it right after this check and adding/attaching to container view with id `@+iditems`. So far so good! 2. Second Call: Upon calling the code block again without changing anything in your layout file or FragmentActivity class definition (i..e., no additional XML layouts), setContentView will reuse whatever was inflated previously—in this case, just one FrameLayout with id @+id/items still associated from before; hence when doing findFragmentByTag("MyListfragment"), now gets a non-null value since you added it during the first call. texts.full_text: (230 mosmol/kg), reduced (105 mosmol/kg, low NaCl) or increased osmolality (550 mosmol/kg, Na-Gluconate added). Altered perilymphatic tonicity did not produce significant changes in endolymphatic ionic concentrations during up to 5 min. In the presence of hypotonic perilymph, decreased osmolality, K and Cl concentrations were observed at 10 min. In the presence of hypertonic perilymph, the endolymphatic osmolality began to increase at 5 min and by 10 min Na concentration had also significantly increased. On decreasing the tonicity of the external solution an immediate decline was observed in transepithelial potential, whereas hypertonicity produced the opposite effect. In the intact frog labyrinth, mEPSPs and spike potentials were recorded from single fibers of the posterior nerve in normal Ringer's (240 mosmol/kg) as well as in solutions with modified tonicity. Hypotonic solutions consistently decreased and hypertonic solutions consistently increased mEPSP and spike frequencies, independent of the species whose concentration was altered. These effects ensued within 1-2 min after the start of perfusion with the test solutions. In particular, when the tonicity was changed by varying Na concentration the mean mEPSP rate was directly related to osmolality. Size histograms of synaptic potentials were well described by single log-normal distribution functions under all experimental conditions. Hypotonic solutions (105 mosmol/kg) markedly shifted the histograms to the left. Hypertonic solutions (380-550 mosmol/kg, NaCl or Na-Gluconate added) shifted the histograms to the right. Hypertonic solutions obtained by adding sucrose to normal Ringer's solution (final osmolality 550 mosmol/kg) increased mEPSP and spike rates, but did not display appreciable effects on mEPSP size. All effects on spike discharge and on mEPSP rate and size were rapidly reversible. In Ca-free, 10 mM EGTA, Ringer's solution, the sensory discharge was completely abolished and did not recover on making the solution hypertonic. These results indicate that perilymphatic solutions with altered tonicity produce small and slowly ensuing changes in the transepithelial parameters which may indirectly affect the sensory discharge rate, whereas relevant, early and reversible effects occur at the cytoneural junction. texts.full_text: This contrasting pattern between urinary and circulating TFF2 suggests a role for this protein in renal function monitoring. Human Trefoil Factor 2 is known to interact specifically within gastrointestinal tissues where it supports mucosal repair, but recent studies have highlighted its potential extraintestinal functions as well: 1. Role of Extracellular Matrix: Studies suggest that human trefoil factor-2 can modulate the structure and functionality of gastric epithelial cell layers by interacting with components like galectin 3 (LGALS3) to facilitate tissue repair. 2. Inflammatory Response Regulation: - TFF2 has been shown to interact with toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4), a key mediator in innate immunity, which can influence the inflammatory response. 3. Kidney Function: As mentioned earlier, changes in serum and urine levels of TFF2 correlate inversely during chronic kidney disease progression: - Decreasing urinary excretion may indicate impaired renal function or reduced synthesis due to epithelial damage, - Increasing blood concentrations could reflect a compensatory mechanism for maintaining mucosal integrity elsewhere. 4. Cancer Biology: Emerging evidence points towards possible involvement of TFF2 in various cancer types, particularly those affecting the gastrointestinal tract: Interactions with proteins such as annexin A1 (ANXA1) have been noted to affect cell proliferation and migration patterns. 5. Neuroprotective Effects: - There is also some preliminary data indicating that trefoil factors might play a role in neuroprotection through interactions not yet fully characterized. texts.full_text: the cost of destroying our environment Dhaka has ranked consistently as one of the most polluted cities in the world, with its air classified as being “unhealthy.” It should come as no surprise that amongst the top 10 causes of death in the country, five of them are lung cancer, lower respiratory tract infections, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, ischemic heart disease, and stroke, all of which are related to air pollution. Despite this, governmental policies and behaviour are yet to reflect the seriousness with which such a state of affairs should be handled. This is why the latest draft of the Clean Air Act 2019, which proposes taking a hardline stance against air pollution, is something the entire nation should get behind. We can no longer afford to have the very environment we live in be tainted by the negligent behaviours related to infrastructural improvement, no matter how important they might be economically or politically; it's about safeguarding our health and wellbeing now for future generations too - because once polluted air is cleaned up – getting rid of pollution like that can take years if not decades in some cases. So what needs doing urgently? Well first off, there must be increased efforts towards implementing stricter standards on industrial emissions as well public education programmes focusing heavily upon making people more aware about the perils associated with polluting our air - whether it's via waste burning or driving vehicles contributing to toxic levels of particulate matter and harmful gases. Donald Manning pleaded guilty in federDonald Manning pleaded guilty in federal court on charges of running a Ponzi scheme that defrauded investors, mostly retirees, out of millions of dollars. Manning, along with two co-defendants, was indicted on charges of running a fraudulent investment scheme that promised high returns on investments, primarily targeting retirees. The scheme, which operated under the guise of a legitimate investment firm, was revealed to be a Ponzi scheme, where the returns paid to early investors were funded by the investments of later investors, creating a pyramid-like structure that was bound to collapse. The collapse of the scheme left many retirees, who had invested their life savings, facing significant financial losses. The investigation into the scheme revealed a pattern of deception, where the perpetrators used a variety of tactics to lure investors, including false promises of high returns, fabricated success stories, and false certifications of the scheme's legitimacy. Manning's guilty plea is a significant development in the case, as it brings the total number of defendants who have pleaded guilty to two. The case is ongoing, and the remaining defendants are expected to face trial in the near future. The case serves as a reminder of the importance of exercising caution when investing, particularly in schemes that promise unusually high returns. It is a reminder that high returns often come with high risks, and that investors should always do their due diligence and seek professional advice before investing. The case also highlights the need for stronger regulatory frameworks and more effective mechanisms for detecting and preventing such schemes.
